perturbation.original_text: Franchisee shall make the funds available to Pretzel Time for withdrawal by electronic transfer no later than one day prior to the due date for payment therefor. The Royalty and Advertising Fund Fees amount actually transferred from Franchisee's account shall be based on the Unit's Net Revenues indicated on the reports submitted by Franchisee as required hereunder. If Franchisee has not reported the Unit's Net Revenues to Pretzel Time for any week as required herein, then Pretzel Time shall be authorized to debit Franchisee's account in an amount equal to the fees transferred from Franchisee's account for the last reporting period for which a report of the Unit's Net Revenues was provided to Pretzel Time as required hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Franchisee shall make the funds available to Pretzel Time for withdrawal by electronic transfer no later than seven days prior to the due date for payment therefor. The Royalty and Advertising Fund Fees amount actually transferred from Franchisee's account shall be based on the Unit's Net Revenues indicated on the reports submitted by Franchisee as required hereunder. If Franchisee has not reported the Unit's Net Revenues to Pretzel Time for any week as required herein, then Pretzel Time shall be authorized to debit Franchisee's account in an amount equal to three times the fees transferred from Franchisee's account for the last reporting period for which a report of the Unit's Net Revenues was provided to Pretzel Time as required hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: This change increases the withdrawal window to seven days before the due date and authorizes a debit of three times the usual amount, potentially violating consumer protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Fund Transfer Act (EFTA) and Regulation E
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially violates EFTA and Regulation E because it allows unauthorized and excessive debits from the franchisee's account. The EFTA requires clear authorization for electronic fund transfers and provides procedures for error resolution. Debiting three times the usual amount without explicit agreement contradicts these protections, as does requiring money to be available 7 days prior to the due date, effectively depriving the franchisee of revenue.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces inconsistencies that create a legal contradiction. The modified text allows Pretzel Time to debit three times the usual amount if the franchisee fails to report net revenues. This contradicts the EFTA and Regulation E as explained in the Law Explanation because it potentially allows unauthorized and excessive debits from the franchisee's account, which undermines the consumer protection provided by EFTA, requiring clear authorization for electronic fund transfers. Although the scraped snippets are limited, the Law Explanation asserts a violation of EFTA and Regulation E due to potentially unauthorized debits. This supports the determination of a contradiction because the new debiting policy could be viewed as unauthorized under EFTA regulations.

perturbation.original_text: If Franchisee is in substantial compliance with this Agreement and Pretzel Time substantially breaches a material provision of this Agreement and (1) fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after written notice thereof is delivered to Pretzel Time or (2) if such breach cannot reasonably be cured within thirty (30) days after Pretzel Time's receipt of such notice, undertake (within thirty (30) days after Pretzel Time's receipt of such notice and continue until completion), reasonable efforts to cure such breach, Franchisee may terminate this Agreement. Such termination shall be effective ten (10) days after delivery to Pretzel Time of notice that such breach has not been cured and Franchisee elects to terminate this Agreement. A termination of this Agreement by Franchisee for any reason other than a substantial breach of a material provision of this Agreement by Pretzel Time, and Pretzel Time's failure to cure such breach as provided above shall be deemed a termination by Franchisee without cause.
perturbation.changed_text: If Franchisee is in substantial compliance with this Agreement and Pretzel Time substantially breaches a material provision of this Agreement and (1) fails to cure such breach within fifteen (15) days after written notice thereof is delivered to Pretzel Time or (2) if such breach cannot reasonably be cured within fifteen (15) days after Pretzel Time's receipt of such notice, undertake (within fifteen (15) days after Pretzel Time's receipt of such notice and continue until completion), reasonable efforts to cure such breach, Franchisee may terminate this Agreement. Such termination shall be effective five (5) days after delivery to Pretzel Time of notice that such breach has not been cured and Franchisee elects to terminate this Agreement. A termination of this Agreement by Franchisee for any reason other than a substantial breach of a material provision of this Agreement by Pretzel Time, and Pretzel Time's failure to cure such breach as provided above shall be deemed a termination by Franchisee without cause.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 15 days and the termination effectiveness to 5 days may be considered unreasonable and unconscionable, conflicting with contract law principles of fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification reduces the time a franchisee has to address and resolve breaches by the franchisor. This shortened timeframe could be deemed unconscionable under UCC § 2-302, especially if the franchisee is put at a severe disadvantage by the shortened period. The cure period could be found to be too short to provide a meaningful opportunity to remedy the breach, making the clause unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the cure period from 30 days to 15 days and the termination effectiveness from 10 days to 5 days. This contradicts the principle of 'unconscionable contract or clause' as described in UCC § 2-302, cited in Scraped Law Snippet 1. The shortened timeframe may not provide a reasonable opportunity to remedy the breach, potentially disadvantaging the franchisee, as indicated in the Law Explanation. The modification could be deemed unconscionable if it leads to an unfair or oppressive outcome.

perturbation.original_text: Failure to open the Unit within the aforementioned time period shall result in the termination of this Franchise Agreement and all franchise fees paid by Franchisee shall be nonrefundable. Franchisee agrees not to open the Unit for business until the following has occurred: (1). Pretzel Time approves the Unit pursuant to its Pre-Opening Checklist; (2). Pre-opening training of Franchisee and Unit personnel has been completed to Pretzel Time's satisfaction; (3). The initial franchise fee and all other amounts then due to Pretzel Time have been paid in full; (4). Pretzel Time has been furnished with copies of all insurance policies required by this Agreement, or such other evidence of insurance coverage and payment of premiums as Pretzel Time requests; and (5) Franchisee has executed Pretzel Time's wire transfer agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Failure to open the Unit within the aforementioned time period shall result in the termination of this Franchise Agreement and all franchise fees paid by Franchisee shall be nonrefundable, even if Pretzel Time is responsible for the delay. Franchisee agrees not to open the Unit for business until the following has occurred: (1). Pretzel Time approves the Unit pursuant to its Pre-Opening Checklist, regardless of external factors; (2). Pre-opening training of Franchisee and Unit personnel has been completed to Pretzel Time's satisfaction, regardless of personnel availability; (3). The initial franchise fee and all other amounts then due to Pretzel Time have been paid in full, even if Pretzel Time has not fulfilled its obligations; (4). Pretzel Time has been furnished with copies of all insurance policies required by this Agreement, or such other evidence of insurance coverage and payment of premiums as Pretzel Time requests, even if insurance is unavailable; and (5) Franchisee has executed Pretzel Time's wire transfer agreement, even if the agreement is unfair.
perturbation.explanation: The added clauses stating that fees are nonrefundable and obligations must be met even if Pretzel Time or external factors are responsible for delays could be deemed unconscionable and violate implied duties of good faith.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By explicitly stating that the franchise fees are nonrefundable even if Pretzel Time is responsible for the failure to open the unit, and obligating the franchisee to fulfill requirements regardless of external factors or fairness, the changed text violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Section 205 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts implies that each contract imposes upon each party a duty of good faith and fair dealing in its performance and its enforcement. The modified language attempts to absolve Pretzel Time of responsibility for delays and imposes unfair obligations on the franchisee, which undermines the duty of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces clauses that explicitly state the franchisee's obligations must be met even if Pretzel Time or external factors are responsible for delays, and that fees are nonrefundable under those circumstances. This directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The LLM-generated law explanation specifies that Section 205 of the Restatement (Second) of Contracts implies a duty of good faith, which the modified language undermines by absolving Pretzel Time of responsibility and imposing unfair obligations. Scraped snippet 2, though incomplete, references 'RESTATEMENT, SECOND, TORTS', suggesting relevant legal principles are being contravened. The added clauses create a scenario where Pretzel Time could benefit from its own failures while holding the franchisee to the original terms, a clear breach of the implied duty of good faith.
